structured_choices.data_source: Hospital system patient records spanning 24 months. EHR data includes structured fields (age, sex, comorbidities, lab values, vital signs) and unstructured clinical notes. Image data includes radiological studies (X-rays, MRIs) with DICOM metadata. All records are linked by unique patient identifiers and timestamped at order/acquisition time. Data is sourced from a single hospital network to control for systematic variation in imaging protocols and diagnostic workflows.
structured_choices.label_definition: Disease diagnosis labels are derived from confirmed diagnostic codes (ICD-10) assigned by clinicians at the point of confirmed diagnosis (typically via imaging report, biopsy, or clinical follow-up confirmation). Labels are assigned post-hoc at the time of diagnosis confirmation to ensure ground truth. A single reference standard (e.g., pathology result, radiologist consensus reading, or clinical documentation) is used per patient per disease. Cases with conflicting or missing confirmatory evidence are excluded from the labeled dataset.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal + stratified split. The first 18 months of data (chronologically earliest patient episodes) are used for training; months 19–21 are used for validation (model selection); months 22–24 are held out as the test set. Within each split, stratification by disease class ensures proportional representation across train/val/test. This temporal approach prevents temporal leakage (future information informing past predictions) and reflects real-world deployment where the model must generalize to future patient cohorts. Stratification within each temporal window handles class imbalance.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit on the training set only. Fitted parameters (mean/std for scaling, vocabulary for text, image normalization statistics) are then applied identically to validation and test sets. Image normalization (e.g., DICOM window/level, resizing) uses population statistics derived from the training set. No data from validation or test sets informs any preprocessing step.
structured_choices.features: Text features: Structured EHR fields (age, sex, lab values) are standardized numerically. Clinical notes are processed via TF-IDF vectorization with stop-word removal and n-grams (1–2), fitted on training notes only. Image features: Raw DICOM images are normalized using training-set statistics (Hounsfield unit scaling for CT/MRI, intensity normalization for X-ray), resized to 256×256, and passed through a pre-trained convolutional base (e.g., ResNet-50 trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned during model training) to extract 2048-dimensional feature vectors per image. Structured fields and text vectors are concatenated with image embeddings to form a unified feature matrix. No features are constructed from the label itself or from data temporally after diagnosis confirmation.
structured_choices.model: A multi-modal ensemble architecture: (1) A text-only baseline model—logistic regression or gradient boosting (XGBoost) trained on structured EHR fields and TF-IDF text features. (2) A multi-modal model—a neural network with three branches: a dense network on structured fields, a text embedding branch (learned via attention or LSTM on clinical notes), and a CNN branch (ResNet-50 fine-tuned on image data with early stopping on validation loss). Branches are concatenated and passed through 2–3 dense layers with dropout (0.3–0.5) before multi-class softmax output. Both models are trained using class-weighted cross-entropy loss to account for class imbalance.
structured_choices.baseline: The baseline is a text-only model trained on all available EHR data (structured fields + clinical notes vectorized identically) using the same training set, validation procedure, and hyperparameter tuning effort as the multi-modal model. The baseline uses logistic regression (L2 regularization tuned on validation set) or XGBoost (depth, learning rate tuned on validation set). Both baseline and multi-modal models are tuned via random search or Bayesian optimization over equivalent hyperparameter budgets. The baseline receives the same class weighting and cross-entropy loss. This ensures that performance differences are attributable to the addition of image data, not to model complexity or tuning asymmetry.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Macro-averaged F1 score (average F1 across all disease classes, unweighted). Justification: In a multi-class diagnostic setting with class imbalance and high stakes (misdiagnosis of rare diseases can be catastrophic), macro-F1 ensures that model performance on minority classes is not masked by high accuracy on common diseases. This aligns with the stakeholder objective of 'improving diagnostic accuracy' for all disease classes equally, particularly those that are underrepresented but clinically important. Macro-F1 penalizes both false negatives (missed diagnoses) and false positives (over-diagnosis) uniformly.
secondary_metrics: Weighted F1 score (weighted by class prevalence) — reflects overall diagnostic utility in the patient population
Per-class precision and recall — enables identification of which diseases the multi-modal model improves or degrades relative to the baseline
AUROC (macro-averaged) — measures ranking ability and robustness to threshold changes
Balanced accuracy (average of per-class recall) — another imbalance-aware metric to validate macro-F1 findings
Calibration (Brier score, expected calibration error) — if predicted probabilities are used for clinical decision support, calibration is critical
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both baseline and multi-modal models are selected using the validation set (months 19–21) via random search or Bayesian optimization. Macro-F1 on the validation set is the selection criterion. Early stopping (for neural network models) is applied during training if validation loss does not improve for 10 consecutive epochs. The test set is not used during any tuning, feature selection, or threshold optimization.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 22–24) is held out and untouched during all model development, tuning, and validation. After the baseline and multi-modal models are finalized (fixed hyperparameters, fixed architecture), predictions are generated on the test set exactly once. Metrics are computed on these held-out predictions and reported without further optimization or thresholding adjustments. Test set results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped) to quantify uncertainty.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Clinician variation (diagnostic practice heterogeneity) is controlled by: (1) stratifying by clinician or department during the train/val/test split to ensure each split contains a representative mix; (2) including clinician identifier or department as a categorical feature in the model to allow the model to learn and adjust for systematic biases; (3) in sensitivity analysis, evaluating model performance separately by clinician/department to detect if improvement is driven by specific subgroups. Imaging protocol variation is controlled by: (1) normalizing images using training-set statistics; (2) including imaging modality (X-ray vs. MRI vs. CT) as a feature; (3) stratifying data by modality during splits if modality is highly predictive. Population drift between training and test periods is minimized by the temporal split design and evaluated via a distribution shift test (e.g., Maximum Mean Discrepancy on feature distributions).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal vs. Text-Only Disease Diagnosis

### 1. Data Acquisition and Labeling

We would source patient records from the hospital system spanning 24 months of patient episodes. The dataset includes structured EHR fields (demographics, laboratory values, vital signs), unstructured clinical notes, and linked radiological images (DICOM format). All records are aligned by unique patient identifier and timestamped at the point of imaging or clinical order.

Disease labels are derived from confirmed diagnostic codes (ICD-10) established post-hoc at the point of clinical confirmation (e.g., pathology result, radiologist consensus reading, or documented clinical diagnosis). Cases lacking confirmatory evidence or with conflicting labels are excluded. This ensures ground-truth labels reflect actual clinical outcomes, not provisional assessments.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy

We divide the 24-month dataset chronologically: months 1–18 form the training set, months 19–21 the validation set, and months 22–24 the test set. This temporal split prevents temporal leakage—the model cannot learn from future information—and reflects real-world deployment where the model must generalize to subsequent patient cohorts.

Within each temporal window, we apply stratification by disease class to ensure each split maintains proportional representation. This addresses class imbalance: if a rare disease comprises 5% of the population, it comprises approximately 5% of each split, preventing the model from learning a trivial bias toward common classes.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured EHR fields (age, sex, lab values) are standardized using z-score normalization. Clinical notes are vectorized using TF-IDF with bigrams (1–2 grams) and English stop-word removal. All preprocessing parameters (scaling statistics, vocabulary) are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without modification.

Radiological images are normalized using training-set DICOM statistics (Hounsfield unit normalization for CT/MRI; intensity scaling for X-ray). Images are resized to 256×256 pixels. Features are extracted via a ResNet-50 convolutional base (pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned during training) yielding 2048-dimensional embeddings per image. Structured fields, text TF-IDF vectors, and image embeddings are concatenated into a unified feature matrix. No features encode the label or post-diagnosis information.

### 4. Baseline and Model Selection

The baseline model is text-only: logistic regression or XGBoost trained on structured EHR fields and clinical note TF-IDF vectors. The baseline receives identical preprocessing, class weighting, and hyperparameter tuning effort as the multi-modal model. Both models are tuned via Bayesian optimization over equivalent budgets (e.g., 50 iterations) on the validation set, selecting hyperparameters that maximize macro-F1.

The multi-modal model is a neural network with three branches: (1) dense layers on structured fields, (2) learned text embeddings via attention over note tokens, (3) CNN fine-tuning on images. Branches merge via concatenation and pass through 2–3 dense layers (dropout 0.3–0.5) to multi-class softmax output. Both models use class-weighted cross-entropy loss to handle imbalance.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 score. In multi-class diagnosis with imbalance and high stakes (missing rare-disease diagnoses is costly), macro-F1 ensures model performance on minority classes is not masked by accuracy on common diseases. It reflects the stakeholder objective: improving diagnostic accuracy for all disease classes, especially underrepresented ones.

Secondary metrics include weighted F1, per-class precision/recall, macro-AUROC, balanced accuracy, and calibration (Brier score). Per-class metrics enable diagnosis-specific improvement assessment.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

During development, hyperparameters are selected on the validation set (months 19–21) using macro-F1. Early stopping is applied during neural network training if validation loss stalls for 10 epochs. The test set is sealed and untouched until final evaluation.

After both models are finalized, predictions are generated on the test set exactly once. Metrics are computed on these held-out predictions. Results are reported with 95% bootstrapped confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty. No threshold adjustments or post-hoc tuning on the test set is permitted.

### 7. Confound Control

Clinician diagnostic practice variation is controlled by: (1) stratifying train/val/test by clinician/department to ensure representation; (2) including clinician/department as a categorical feature; (3) performing sensitivity analysis to detect department-specific effects. Imaging protocol variation is minimized by normalizing using training statistics and including modality as a feature. Population drift is assessed via distribution shift tests on feature distributions between training and test periods.

### 8. Scope

This design establishes whether multi-modal data *improves diagnostic accuracy* (macro-F1) beyond text-only EHR data, controlling for model complexity and tuning effort. It does not establish causality or generalizability beyond the hospital system or time period studied.
